summary: Gruber examines the backlash against Apple's Final Cut Pro X release, which professional editors criticized as more of an improved iMovie than a true successor to Final Cut Pro 7. He draws an analogy to the Mac OS 9-to-OS X transition, noting that Apple provided years of backward compatibility then but abruptly discontinued legacy Final Cut Pro versions this time. He acknowledges the ground-up rewrite may have been the right long-term move but argues Apple erred by not offering a transition period. He curates reactions ranging from harsh criticism to cautious optimism, and predicts Apple will eventually provide a two-year transition window while rebuilding missing professional features.
key_points: FCP X lacks critical professional features including tape output, EDL/XML export, OMF output, and the ability to import FCP 7 projects
The Mac OS X transition analogy: Apple kept OS 9 around for years with Classic emulation, but discontinued legacy Final Cut Pro immediately upon FCP X's release
Even iMovie HD 6 got a two-year transition period as a free download when Apple redesigned iMovie, making the lack of transition for professionals even more puzzling
Professional reactions range from calling it the worst product launch ever to praising its speed and arguing FCP 7 still works fine in the meantime
Gruber predicts Apple will eventually follow the iMovie and Mac OS X pattern with a two-year transition period
money_quotes: The gist is that many professional editors see Final Cut Pro X as an improved version of iMovie, not an improved version of Final Cut Pro.

A true ground-up rewrite with the intention of laying a solid foundation for the long-term future, but, in the short term, lots of missing features and frustrating changes compared to what current users were accustomed.

This ground-up rewrite may well have been the right thing to do. Apple seems convinced that this is a better fundamental concept for video editing — and, really, storytelling in general. But it may prove risky not to offer a transition period.
If iMovie users were worth appeasing with a transition period, surely professional Final Cut Pro users are too.
If Final Cut Pro X can't even open Final Cut Pro 7 projects, how quickly can editors be expected to switch?

I predict this transition going the way of iMovie's (and, again, Mac OS X's) — with a two-year transition where the previous Final Cut Pro suite remains available, whilst the new Final Cut Pro X suite regains lost features.
key_insight: Apple's willingness to break backward compatibility for a better long-term foundation is defensible, but refusing to offer any transition period for professional users who depend on legacy workflows is an unforced error.
